chemicals.description: in Cats • Systemic Effects: The sources describe a toxicity that affects the central nervous system and cardiovascular system. • Clinical Manifestations: Ingestion leads to behavioral changes, disorientation, and dilated pupils. • Physiological Changes: The toxins cause hyperthermia (elevated body temperature) and alter vital signs, specifically causing changes in heart rate and respiratory rate.
chemicals.name: • Source Information: The provided texts do not explicitly name the toxins

description: Angel's Trumpets (Brugmansia spp., and the closely related Datura) is one of the most dangerous ornamental plants a cat can encounter. Every part of the plant — flowers, leaves, stems, seeds, and roots — contains high concentrations of tropane alkaloids, primarily atropine, scopolamine, and hyoscyamine. These compounds work by blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, the receptors the nervous system uses to control glands, smooth muscle, and heart rate. When these receptors are blocked, the body's entire parasympathetic nervous system is effectively switched off, producing what veterinarians call anticholinergic syndrome. Although the plant's strong scent deters many cats, ingestion still occurs — and because cats cannot sweat, the disruption to their thermoregulatory system makes hyperthermia (dangerously high body temperature) a particularly life-threatening complication.
name: Angel's Trumpets
safetyNotes: Symptom progression: Early signs include dilated pupils, rapid heart rate, dry mouth, and restlessness. As toxicity progresses: agitation, disorientation, inability to urinate, and muscle tremors. Severe cases escalate to hyperthermia, seizures, respiratory failure, and coma.
Seeds and leaves carry the highest alkaloid load — but all parts, including the nectar and sap, are dangerous. Even second-hand exposure (e.g. a cat grooming pollen from its coat) can cause symptoms.
Hyperthermia risk: Because cats cannot sweat, the loss of thermoregulation from anticholinergic syndrome can rapidly push body temperature to fatal levels, especially in warm environments.
Action: This is a veterinary emergency requiring immediate treatment. Do not wait for symptoms to worsen — bring the cat to a vet at once, ideally with a sample of the plant for identification.

symptoms.name: Behavioral changes and Disorientation
symptoms.notes: The plant affects the nervous system, leading to noticeable changes in behavior and confusion.Dilated Pupils

symptoms.name: Dilated Pupils (Mydriasis)
symptoms.notes: This is a hallmark sign of the plant's toxicity, often associated with the alkaloids present in the plant.Hyperthermia

symptoms.name: Hyperthermia
symptoms.notes: Ingestion causes a dangerous elevation in body temperature.Cardiovascular Instability

symptoms.name: Change in heart rate
symptoms.notes: The toxins alter vital signs, specifically affecting cardiac rhythm and rate.Respiratory Distress

symptoms.name: Change in respiratory rate
symptoms.notes: Alongside cardiac changes, the respiratory rate is significantly altered, necessitating immediate veterinary intervention.Gastrointestinal Upset

symptoms.name: Loss of appetite
symptoms.notes: While neurological and cardiac signs are prominent, the cat may also refuse food.

treatments.description: Seek veterinary care immediately if ingestion is suspected. Do not wait for symptoms to appear. The plant is noted to cause severe systemic effects that can escalate quickly.
treatments.name: Emergency Veterinary Consultation/Examination
treatments.notes: Angel's Trumpets produce beautiful blooms but are highly toxic. Although their strong odor often deters pets, ingestion still occurs and affects vital systems (heart and lungs).Management of Hyperthermia

treatments.description: Veterinary staff will monitor and manage the cat's body temperature.
treatments.name: Thermoregulation / Cooling Measures
treatments.notes: One of the distinct symptoms of Angel's Trumpet poisoning is hyperthermia (elevated body temperature), which requires stabilization to prevent organ damage.Cardiovascular and Respiratory Support

treatments.description: Continuous monitoring of the heart rate and respiratory rate to manage instability.
treatments.name: Cardiac and Respiratory Monitoring
treatments.notes: The toxins in the plant cause significant changes in heart rate and respiratory rate. Veterinary treatment will focus on stabilizing these vital signs to prevent fatal complications.Management of Neurological Symptoms

treatments.description: Managing behavioral changes and preventing injury due to disorientation.
treatments.name: Neurological Support / Seizure Precautions
treatments.notes: Cats may exhibit disorientation, dilated pupils, and behavioral changes due to the plant's effects on the central nervous system.Important Note on "Nightshade" Confusion: While Angel's Trumpet belongs to the Solanaceae (Nightshade) family, it should not be confused with the "Nightshade plants" (like
